data.title: Janus Henderson Responsible International Dividend Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

During the quarter, global equity markets were flat in U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) terms, although international markets declined. Donald Trump’s election win spurred the U.S. market to record highs on hopes the new administration would be more proactive about promoting U.S. economic growth. Elsewhere, concerns that Trump’s policies might negatively impact other countries’ growth due to trade tariffs and changes in foreign policy proved to be a headwind. Political turmoil in Germany and France also weighed on sentiment, with European equities lagging their U.S. counterparts. The “Magnificent 7” stocks – the seven dominant tech companies Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG,GOOGL), META, Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA) – contributed to relative performance, driven by optimism about artificial intelligence. Growth stocks notably outperformed value stocks. Higher yields weighed on defensive sectors such as real estate, consumer staples, and utilities, but were positive for the banking sector, which contributed to relative results. Asian equities weakened over the quarter as anxiety about U.S.-China trade tensions hurt sentiment. Chinese shares retreated amid uncertainty about President-elect Trump’s policies and concerns that stimulus measures would fail to deliver the expected economic boost.

Investors started anticipating fewer interest rate cuts and potentially higher inflation in coming years, with government bond yields rising over the quarter despite rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed), European Central Bank, and the Bank of England. The U.S. dollar strengthened notably over the quarter versus other currencies after the Fed hinted at fewer rate cuts for 2025 than the market expected.

Portfolio review

Within the information technology sector, investor optimism around the adoption of AI drove up the share price of chip manufacturer Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which has benefited from the capital investment deployed by its customers to develop the company’s AI capabilities. This positive contribution to relative performance was partially offset within IT by the portfolio’s holding in Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF), whose share price fell after the company delayed the release of a new semiconductor chip, leading to investor fears that it had missed the AI product cycle.

However, we remain confident the company can recover and believe the stock is attractively valued at current levels.

In the consumer discretionary sector, SONY’s share price benefited from earnings upgrades, driven by the outlook for

its music, games, and films divisions – where in the latter, a number of strong film releases are expected in 2025.

The quarter proved challenging for European financial stocks, with the portfolio’s holding in Dutch bank ING weighing on relative results. Additionally, concerns about Chinese economic growth and the weak domestic consumer environment impacted the holding in Hong Kong-based insurer AIA, which underperformed. This was partially offset by strong performance from our UK bank NatWest, which produced solid returns over 2024, thanks in part to significant restructuring. We believe the company should see further progress in 2025.

Manager outlook

Now that inflation appears to be under control, centrals banks have proceeded to cut interest rates in many countries

around the world, and some governments are looking at ways to stimulate growth. These factors should be positive for companies and consumers as we head into 2025.

However, we remain cognizant of the geopolitical backdrop and recognize that the new U.S. government brings some uncertainty, which could result in market volatility and highlights the importance of maintaining a diversified portfolio.

We remain confident that the companies held in the portfolio can navigate the current economic environment, aided by strong balance sheets and cash-generating capabilities.

Shareholder returns, announced in terms of both dividends and share buybacks, remain positive, which suggests that management teams share our confidence in the long-term outlooks for their companies.

Portfolio management

Ben Lofthouse, CFA | Faizan Baig, CFA

data.title: Janus Henderson Research Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

Equity markets posted further gains in the fourth quarter, following strong performance through the first three quarters of 2024. The U.S. presidential election triggered a strong rally in U.S. stocks. Stocks encountered volatility in December, however, after the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut its policy rate but indicated fewer- than-expected rate reductions for 2025. Economic activity in the U.S. and globally remained relatively solid, while earnings continued to grow for many companies. Meanwhile, major economies outside the U.S. struggled to find their footing, as reflected by mixed economic data across Europe and Asia.

Portfolio review

Relative performance was hindered by our position in ASML Holding (ASML), a leading global supplier of lithography equipment used in semiconductor production. The stock declined in October on near-term revenue growth concerns after the company disclosed weaker-than-expected bookings. It also faced uncertainty over planned capital spending cuts by chipmaker Intel and potential U.S. government restrictions on exports of lithography equipment to China, a major ASML market. The stock regained some ground later in the fourth quarter following a constructive Investor Day, when ASML management gave a positive outlook on the lithography market while providing robust 2030 growth and margin targets. We have continued to see durable competitive advantages for ASML, the sole global supplier of extreme ultraviolet lithography machines used to manufacture the most advanced semiconductor chips.

Building products supplier Ferguson (FERG) was another relative detractor. Ferguson reported weaker-than-expected quarterly results due to weak sales, reduced margins, and increased capital investment. The company also faced challenges as the prospect of higher-than-expected interest rates in 2025 created uncertainty for the construction sector. We believe the market response to its recent results was overstated, and we continue to see long-term opportunity given the company's strong competitive positioning and solid balance sheet.

We benefited from our position in Broadcom (AVGO), a global technology company that designs, develops, and supplies semiconductor and infrastructure software solutions. The company has established a strong track record of robust margins, strong free-cash-flow generation, and best-in-class capital returns. Broadcom has been a beneficiary of increased spending on semiconductors to support the rollout of artificial intelligence. It has also experienced stable revenue growth from other areas of the semiconductor market not driven by AI-related investments. Broadcom reported better-than-expected quarterly earnings and offered a 2027 AI market outlook that significantly exceeded expectations.

Liberty Media, another contributor, owns motor racing company Formula One Group (FWONA). The company has been working to build a higher profile for Formula One racing in the U.S., leading to rapid audience growth and increased consumer engagement. Going forward, we believe the company has multiple avenues for increasing revenue growth and shareholder value as it seeks to renegotiate race promotion fees, sponsorship deals, and global streaming rights on more advantageous terms. We see added opportunity around its planned acquisition of Grand Prix motorcycle racing company MotoGP.

Manager outlook

The combination of recent interest rate cuts and other potential accommodative policies in the U.S. and stimulus in China should lend support to the global economy. Large-cap equity markets have been quick to price in the cycle’s extension, leaving valuations vulnerable to an uneven labor market, the threat of higher long-term rates, and the policy uncertainty that comes with a new U.S. administration.

Meanwhile, major economies outside the U.S. have struggled to find their footing, with mixed data across Europe and Asia. Despite these crosscurrents, investors are entering 2025 in a relatively constructive mood. For our part, we are monitoring pockets of weakness in some subsectors and countries, and we are mindful of the lagged risks of higher interest rates in some areas of consumer credit. Geopolitical developments continue to take a human toll and could potentially have a significant economic impact while contributing to investment market volatility.

We remain committed to our fundamentals-driven investment strategy. This strategy is driven by our strong and experienced analyst team members, who bring deep knowledge of their respective sectors. Their proprietary fundamental research helps identify opportunities that we believe are underappreciated by the market. Our analyst team is constantly on the lookout for companies with strong or improving business models, good management teams, and durable competitive advantages. We continue to find companies with compelling growth opportunities that arise from innovation, market share gains, and strong growth in their end markets. Through this disciplined investment approach, we will continue to pursue our goal of long-term growth in capital.

Portfolio management

Joshua Cummings, CFA | John Jordan

data.title: Eggs, And A Whole Lot More
data.description:
Every election cycle has its own peculiar trope at the center of the narrative, the very tangible thing that brings swirling abstract narratives into recognizably defined three-dimensional space.

For the 2024 campaign, that thing was the price of eggs. The complexities of global supply chains, the arcane accounting practices for billions of dollars of government expenditure, the extreme distortions of consumer demand trends during and after the Covid pandemic – all the many variables involved in the highest levels of inflation experienced since the 1970s boiled down to one simple formulation: Have you seen the price of eggs lately? Yes they had, and they were not happy about it, and they made their point on November 5.

The Chickens and the Eggs

Given the prominent place afforded last year to that one food category as a proxy for the entire inflation story, it is perhaps an unfortunate coincidence that eggs are back in the news recently for reasons unrelated to the inflationary forces at play for much of the past three years.

The spread of avian flu has caused havoc among the chicken farms from which our Grade-A cartons begin their long journey to our kitchen tables. The price of eggs today is roughly double what it was a year ago, and soared by 15 percent in the month of January alone.

The flu has necessitated the slaughter of millions of chickens, drastically reducing supply, and the magnitude of the resulting price increases for eggs accounted for fully two-thirds of the total increase in food prices last month, according to the latest Consumer Price Index report issued by the Bureau of Labor Statistics this past week.

But it’s not just about eggs. The Fed doesn’t pay as much attention to the volatile categories of food and energy products as it does to so-called core inflation, the more stable baskets of goods and services like apparel, medical care and shelter. The problem is, these categories are proving to be stubborn as well.

Gimme Shelter

Non-food and energy services, in particular, have not come down at the rate the Fed was expecting to see a year ago. For the last twelve months, prices for shelter (rent and owner’s equivalent) have increased 4.4 percent while transportation services, of which airline travel is a major component, have gone up 8.0 percent.

That has kept the core CPI number (the gray dotted line in the above chart) stuck at or around 3.3 percent since the middle of last summer. The Fed went ahead with its first rate cut in September regardless, thinking that the numbers would continue to decline, but here we are in February and 3.3 percent is still the number for core inflation.

And That’s Without New Tariffs

The seeming intractability of inflation alone would strongly suggest a continued pause by the Fed, with no rate cuts likely for the foreseeable future. But that, of course, doesn’t take into account whatever tariffs are eventually going to make their way into law.

As we noted in our commentary last week, the only consistent aspect of the new administration’s tariff policy so far is its inconsistency, with policymakers saying one thing one day, watching how markets react, and saying something else the next day (or the next hour).

The word in vogue this week has been “reciprocal tariffs,” which sounds like a sort of tit-for-tat policy aimed at matching the tariffs other countries set on products we currently import.

Or is it? Does it include things like the 20 percent VAT applied to many European exports? How about structural barriers on products from places like Japan and India? And when? The latest indications point to April (maybe) as the likely start date (unless something else comes up in the meantime, and notwithstanding carve-outs and exemptions and all the rest of the baroque ornaments of this administration’s approach to tariffs).

All this remains to be seen, and markets have yet to exhibit any overriding concerns. Meanwhile, interest rates are likely to remain where they are, and vegan baking recipes that call for egg substitutes are likely to grow in popularity.

data.title: Janus Henderson Overseas ADR Managed Account Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

Non-U.S. equities delivered negative returns in the fourth quarter, as a strong dollar, increased geopolitical uncertainty, and a weaker outlook for the Eurozone economy pressured investor sentiment toward international shares. The U.S. presidential election triggered a broad rotation out of international stocks and into the U.S. market, as investors anticipated a strong pro-American agenda and potential protectionist trade policies under Donald Trump’s presidency. The sell-off in non-U.S. stocks was often indiscriminate and left many stocks trading at discounted multiples that we believe do not accurately reflect business prospects or the potential for free-cash-flow growth.

Portfolio review

Stock selection lifted the portfolio’s relative performance in a challenging fourth quarter. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) was a top relative contributor. As the world’s largest contract chip manufacturer, TSMC is a major fabricator of logic semiconductors including graphic processing units critical to the deployment of generative artificial intelligence. As its competitors have faltered, TSMC has built on its market leadership, leading to increased pricing power and margins. Partnerships with large technology companies and hyperscalers have also supported its revenue growth, helping TSMC report even stronger-than-expected earnings and increased guidance.

Liberty Media, another contributor, owns a controlling interest in motor racing company Formula One Group (FWONA). Liberty Media’s efforts to build a higher profile for Formula One racing, especially in the U.S., have resulted in increased viewership and advertising revenues. Going forward, we believe the company may have multiple avenues to grow revenues and shareholder value as it looks to renegotiate race promotion fees, sponsorship deals, and global streaming rights on more advantageous terms. We see added opportunity around the company’s acquisition of Grand Prix motorcycle racing company MotoGP.

Teck Resources (TECK) was a prominent detractor. This Canadian mining company produces copper, zinc, and other materials. Teck struggled against a backdrop of weaker copper prices, slower economic growth in China, and a strong U.S. dollar. Longer term, we see strong demand drivers for copper, a key material required for green energy applications and to meet AI-powered demand for carbon neutral electricity sources. We believe the mining industry will struggle to produce enough copper to meet this demand, leading to higher copper prices. In our view, Teck is well positioned to benefit from these trends due to its attractive production pipeline and relatively low capital requirements.

ICON (ICLR), another detractor, is a multinational provider of outsourced drug and medical device development and clinical testing services. ICON disappointed investors by reporting weaker-than-expected revenue growth, partly because of the cancellation of some new drug development orders. Customers, especially in the biotechnology space, have also been more cautious about their spending on clinical trials, which led ICON to lower guidance. Despite near-term uncertainty, we continue to believe ICON is well positioned to benefit as more drug and medical device companies outsource their drug trials.

Manager outlook

We anticipate U.S. policy under the incoming Trump administration could have significant implications for global investment markets. Some Trump policies, such as corporate tax cuts, may be positive drivers of economic and corporate earnings growth. We see greater uncertainty around other policy proposals, such as higher tariffs aimed at China but potentially Mexico, Canada, and the European Union. Such tariffs may invite retaliation and could worsen inflation and lead to higher interest rates. We anticipate we could see some volatility as investors wait for clarity on what policies the Trump administration will be able to enact, and how such policies might impact the broader global economy.

Rather than try to predict broader fiscal and monetary policy shifts, we will look to use volatility to our advantage as we seek out opportunities to put our bottom-up stock selection to work. We remain focused on identifying pockets of opportunity around the world where we believe fundamentals and free-cash-flow growth are not fully reflected in stock valuations. We have identified such opportunities in a diverse range of countries and industries including European and Japanese financials that may benefit from higher-for- longer interest rates. We have also found attractive investments in consumer companies with strong global brands, as well as with manufacturers of key components that support AI. Many of these component makers have been beneficiaries of AI-related spending but trade at much more attractive valuations relative to large, high-profile U.S. technology companies. As we see these opportunities, we continue to pay close attention to the quality of balance sheets, management teams, and corporate strategy. We view this disciplined, fundamentals-driven approach as key to delivering long-term capital appreciation for our investors.

Portfolio management

Julian McManus | Christopher O'Malley, CFA

data.title: FVAL: A High-Tech Value Play With Risky Downside Exposure
data.description:
ETF Overview

Fidelity Value Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:FVAL) invests in a portfolio of about 130 U.S. large-cap and mid-cap value stocks. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.15% is comparable to other factor-based funds. For example, Fidelity Quality Factor ETF (FQAL) has a slightly higher expense ratio of 0.16%. FVAL’s sector neutral approach has resulted in its higher exposure to the technology sector relative to other value funds. This improves its growth profile, but its long-term growth return still trails the S&P 500 index. On the other hand, FVAL is underweight in defensive sectors relative to other value funds, making it more vulnerable in market downturns. Therefore, FVAL appears to be an awkward ETF, as investors seeking higher returns might opt for the S&P 500, while those wanting better downside protection may consider other value funds.

Fund Analysis

FVAL’s construction methodology

Let us look at how FVAL constructs its portfolio. FVAL basically tracks the Fidelity U.S. Value Factor Index. This index selects stocks based on a factor-based value approach. As can be seen from the table below, four value factors that are used to determine which stocks are to be selected include: free cash flow yield (strong cash generation), EBITDA to EV ratio (how efficient the company operates relative to valuation), tangible book value to price (asset-backed valuation), and forward earnings yield (future earnings expectations).

Overweight in technology but underweight in defensive sectors

FVAL’s portfolio construction has resulted in a portfolio of value stocks. But its sector composition is very different than other value funds. Below is a table that shows FVAL’s sector allocation. As can be seen from the table, the technology sector represents about 30.6% of its total portfolio. This is unusually high relative to other traditional value funds. For example, Vanguard Value ETF (VTV) and iShares S&P Core Value ETF (IUSV) have lower technology stock exposures of 11.8% and 23.4% respectively. This is because FVAL tries to maintain sector neutrality relative to the broader market (e.g. S&P 500 index), and technology stocks represent nearly one third of the S&P 500 index. This explains why FVAL has very similar technology sector exposure to the S&P 500 index.

On the other hand, FVAL’s exposure to defensive sectors is quite limited. This is also due to its sector neutrality approach. As the table above shows, consumer defensive, healthcare, utilities sectors represent about 17.9% of its total portfolio. This is similar to the S&P 500 index’s exposure (about 19.1%). In contrast, VTV and IUSV have much higher exposure to defensive sectors at 31.3% and 26.1% respectively.

FVAL has outperformed VTV and IUSV in the past

FVAL has delivered a total return of 191.4% since its inception back in September 2016. In contrast, VTV and IUSV delivered inferior total returns of 150.8% and 148.7% respectively. FVAL’s outperformance in the past was likely due to its higher exposure towards the technology sector. As we know, the technology sector has been one of the fastest growing sectors among all other sectors in the S&P 500 index thanks to technology advancement. The sector is poised to deliver strong growth in the upcoming decades as well, fueled by many technological megatrends such as artificial intelligence, high-performance computing, AR/VRs, robotics, etc. Therefore, it is very likely that FVAL will continue to outperform VTV and IUSV in the foreseeable future.

Now that some might ask whether FVAL will match the performance of the S&P 500 index given its similar exposure to technology stock. The answer is likely not. This is because FVAL’s portfolio is tilted towards value and has fewer growth stocks than the S&P 500 index. As can be seen from the chart below, value stocks represent about 44% of FVAL’s portfolio, while growth stocks only represent about 8%. In contrast, growth stocks represent about 21% of the S&P 500 index, while value stocks only represent about 27% of the index. Therefore, FVAL still has a weaker growth profile than the S&P 500 index.

We can also observe this in the past, where the S&P 500 index’s total return of 224.5% outperformed FVAL’s 191.4% since FVAL’s inception.

FVAL has higher downside risk

FVAL has been through 3 major corrections since its inception: late 2018, 2020, and 2022. As can be seen from the chart below, the fund has typically fallen more than its peer funds VTV and IUSV. Therefore, FVAL’s construction methodology does not result in better downside protection. In terms of volatility, FVAL has a beta ratio of 0.98 relative to the S&P 500 index. For reader’s information, a beta ratio above 1 means the fund is more volatile than the S&P 500 index. In contrast, VTV and IUSV have beta ratios of 0.83 and 0.92 respectively.

FVAL’s higher downside risk is not difficult to explain. As we know, many technology stocks are heavily influenced by market sentiments, and as expectations change, they tend to experience higher volatilities, and this may lead to higher downside risk. Therefore, FVAL's exposure to technology stocks will inevitably result in its greater volatility and higher downside risk.

Investor Takeaway

FVAL’s significant exposure to technology stocks has driven its outperformance and improved its growth outlook relative to other value funds. However, FVAL is also vulnerable in market downturns. This makes FVAL a somewhat awkward ETF to own. For investors seeking higher returns, why not simply invest in the S&P 500 funds? For investors wanting better downside protection in market downturns, many other value funds appear to be better choices. Therefore, we see no real advantage of owning FVAL over the S&P 500 index and other value funds.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: Dayforce: Finding Success In Penetrating Upmarket And AI Adoption
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a buy rating for Dayforce (NYSE:DAY) as its accelerating enterprise expansion and AI innovations position it well to continue growing. So long as DAY can continue its current pace of execution, I think it can minimally grow 10% without issues. And if the macro situation gets better, growth could easily surpass my growth assumption.

Business description

DAY is a cloud-based human capital management [HCM] software solution provider. Like many other solution providers, DAY's solution covers a wide range of functions, including payroll, workforce management, and talent acquisition. DAY reports its business in two key segments: cloud revenue and bureau recurring (float income is included here). Of which, cloud revenue represents ~95.7% of total revenue, while the latter represents ~3.9% of total revenue as of 4Q24.

4Q24 earnings

DAY 4Q24 total revenue grew 17% y/y constant currency [CC] to $465.2 million (or 16.4% y/y on a reported basis). This quarter highlighted a significant inflection in sales momentum. After experiencing elongated sales cycles in prior quarters, management noted that deal cycles normalized in 4Q24, and this has helped DAY to achieve its strongest bookings quarter in company history. For reference, January 2025 bookings were more than double the total revenue growth rate, which is a very encouraging sign that demand is accelerating into the new year.

Aside from top-line growth, profitability trends were noteworthy. Adj EBITDA margin expanded to 27.8%, up 300 bps y/y, which demonstrates the strong operating leverage in DAY’s business and the positive margin impact from a higher mix of cloud recurring revenue. This has directly driven up DAY free cash flow [FCF] generation ability elsewhere, wherein FCF margin reached 9.7%, up from 6.9% a year ago.

Enterprise Expansion Driving Larger Deal Sizes

I believe DAY has a bright growth outlook ahead of it, and one that can last for multiple years. The notable aspect that led me to this conclusion is that DAY is finding success in penetrating into the enterprise segment. Historically, DAY's focus was on the mid-market, but in recent quarters, there has been an evident shift towards larger customers. Take, for instance, in 4Q24, DAY secured several high-profile enterprise deals, including (1) a global aerospace firm selecting Dayforce Payroll and Time & Attendance for 18,000 employees; (2) a construction equipment manufacturer implementing Managed Payroll and Time solutions for 6,500 employees; and (3) a US retail cooperative adopting the full Dayforce suite for 66,000 employees across 362 stores.

This shift is meaningful for two reasons. First, enterprise customers tend to generate higher contract values and have higher retention rates. Second, enterprise deals often include multiple modules beyond core payroll, such as workforce management, talent management, and analytics, which increase the average revenue per customer. Collectively, this shift leads to high customer lifetime value and better revenue visibility. This shift is evident if readers were to look at the employee-per-customer metric, which has increased from ~938 in 4Q21 to 1.1k in 4Q24, clearly reflecting a steady move upmarket. This came with DAY improving the revenue retention rate from 96% in 4Q20 to 98% in 4Q24.

It goes without saying that the DAY solution must have worked; if not, large enterprises would not have adopted it. But I think another factor that enabled DAY to penetrate effectively is the industry recognition that it receives, which reinforces its positioning as a top-tier HCM provider. Recently, DAY was named a Leader in the IDC MarketScape for Worldwide Cloud-Enabled HCM and recognized by Nucleus Research for Workforce Management and Talent Acquisition.

With larger enterprises increasingly prioritizing cloud-based HCM solutions, I believe DAY is very well-positioned to capture more demand moving forward.

AI Innovation Expanding Monetization Opportunities

More and more enterprises are looking to adopt AI solutions, simply because it can enhance productivity by a ton, and any HCM solution provider that does not have such an AI solution in its arsenal will lose out to competitors that do (in my view). Hence, I like that management has allocated resources to ensure DAY stays “in the race.”. Management has been actively embedding AI into the Dayforce platform. Notably, DAY’s Co-Pilot, a gen-AI assistant, was launched in November 2024 and has already seen strong traction, with more than 60 units sold within weeks of its debut. The AI-powered demand forecasting tool, which is expected to help businesses better predict workforce needs, is another good example.

Although the revenue impact of these innovations is not visible yet, I think it is only a matter of time as adoption ramps up. The other implication of this is that it makes DAY’s platform a lot stickier since customers are using more of DAY’s solutions, and DAY is increasingly embedded into clients’ workflows. In simpler words, DAY’s AI innovations should drive an increase in spend per customer and retention rates.

Valuation

Without assuming an aggressive growth, I think the upside is alright if DAY can print 10% y/y growth for the next few years (10% is anchored against management's FY25 revenue ex-floating income growth guide and my assumption that floating revenue will stay at the same percentage of total revenue). I do think there is upside potential to my growth estimates, since DAY has just scratched the surface of penetrating the enterprise segment and has shown positive progress in doing so. There is also a secular trend for enterprises looking to shift towards cloud solutions. Along with more AI solutions that enhance DAY’s solution, it is not hard to imagine DAY growing faster than mid-teens growth, especially if we assume a normalization to the US economy in the coming years. But without that acceleration, at a 10% growth outlook, I think the valuation multiple (5.7x forward revenue) will not see a major upward re-rating since Workday trades at 6.9x with a higher growth rate of mid-to-high teens.

Risk

One key risk for DAY is the potential deceleration in recurring revenue growth. The HCM industry is a very competitive one, with large and strong incumbents like Workday and ADP that are actively finding ways to capture share. DAY is also, ultimately, at the mercy of the macro-environment, in that lower employment volumes could hinder revenue growth. Additionally, float revenue is directly impacted by interest rate movements, which could cause a swing in revenue growth.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for DAY as it has demonstrated strong execution in enterprise expansion and rolling out AI solutions, which I believe supports its growth outlook. If DAY continues to penetrate the enterprise market and drive AI adoption, I see a real potential for revenue growth to exceed my expectations. If this happens, the valuation multiple could go higher as the market value gets closer to where Workday trades.

data.title: Fallen Angels Take Flight: A Strong Start To 2025
data.description:
Fallen Angels outperformed high yield in January as lower rates/tighter spreads drove gains. Aptiv joined the index, boosting Auto exposure, while Ford's outlook may signal broader sector headwinds.

Fallen Angels (as represented by the ICE US Fallen Angel High Yield 10% Constrained Index, "H0CF") began 2025 on a positive note, outperforming the broad high-yield market (as represented by the ICE BofA US High Yield Index, "H0A0") by 0.10% (1.48% vs. 1.38%). The outperformance was driven by declining interest rates and tightening credit spreads, particularly in the Real Estate sector, which is the second-largest overweight versus broad high yield, behind Retail. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield fluctuated throughout the month, peaking at 4.79% before settling at 4.52%. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) held interest rates steady in January and is expected to maintain this stance until the summer, although we anticipate that expectations will continue to adjust based on new data points. Lower-rated bonds continued last year's trend of leading performance, with the CCC & below index returning 1.64%, compared to 1.42% for single-B rated bonds and 1.29% for BB-rated bonds.

Fallen Angels are off to a strong start this year. We believe interest rates will remain a key driver of returns in 2025, as they currently account for approximately 65% of total yield. Additionally, credit spreads can remain tight for extended periods, as corporate fundamentals take time to shift. Since 2003, there have been 6 periods-lasting between 0.4 and 3.9 years-where high-yield spreads remained below their historical average and fallen angels outperformed by approximately 3%. Fallen Angels have underperformed by 2% during the current period, which has now reached 2.5 years. This makes it an outlier compared to historical trends. However, the current tight spread period is not over, and wider spreads would likely reflect a changing credit environment in which fallen angels could benefit, for example due to a higher volume of new fallen angels, higher quality or evolving sector themes.

Fitch states that the downgrade aligns more closely with other similarly junior subordinated debt from other issuers. Over the last six months, the junior subordinated debt price decreased to $99.71 from $101.05. Within the Auto sector, S&P affirmed Ford's BBB- rating in early February but revised its outlook to negative, as Ford's expansion over the next couple of years appears to be limited due to slower-than-expected progress on cost reduction, high labor costs and increasing pricing pressure. data.title: Trump's Impact On The Labor Market
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

The Trump administration’s bold policy initiatives to increase government efficiency and tighten immigration will likely have macroeconomic consequences for the labor market, potentially weighing on labor demand and supply.

However, the combined effect will likely also be very nuanced, suggesting the data over the coming months will be difficult to parse and understand, making policymaking even more challenging for the Fed.

The Trump administration has unleashed bold policy initiatives on government efficiency and immigration that are likely to impact the labor market, though in a very nuanced way.

Plans for a wide-scale reduction in government workforce would likely have limited direct impact, especially as the federal workforce only makes up 2% of total nonfarm payrolls. Moreover, while government jobs have increasingly driven payroll gains in recent years - making up 23% of jobs created since August 2022 - only 3% is attributable to the federal government.

Outsourced government work has grown significantly, however, so a reduction in contract spending could pose bigger challenges. Such a reduction would likely dampen labor demand for impacted downstream industries, including professional services, healthcare, and transportation.

Meanwhile, the administration’s goal to implement stricter immigration measures is unlikely to meaningfully impact the flow of labor, especially as immigration flows had already begun normalizing in late-2024.

However, with foreign-born labor making up over half of labor force growth since 2023, increased immigration crackdowns could significantly affect the stock of labor. Tighter immigration policy could reduce labor supply in industries most reliant on immigrant labor: construction, accommodation and food services.

The combined effect of these policies will likely make the labor data over the coming months difficult to parse and understand. Already having to navigate tariff uncertainties, this only makes policymaking even more challenging for the Fed.

data.title: Investing For Security In Unstable Times: Energy And Supply Chains
data.description:
By James MacGregor, CFA | Cem Inal | Luke Pryor

Efforts to secure supply chains and energy sources are creating powerful and enduring themes for equity investors - even in these turbulent times.

Investors are digesting a barrage of US policy decisions and global technology news that has fueled market volatility. Despite the uncertainties, we think some broad trends will ultimately transcend the market noise and offer attractive opportunities for equity investors with a long-term mindset.

Two major forces have unsettled markets in early 2025. Chinese company DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) unveiled a generative artificial intelligence (AI) model that may have been developed at a fraction of the cost of existing models, raising questions about future AI-related spending. Meanwhile, President Trump's plans to impose significant tariffs on major trading partners has triggered extraordinary instability, with the Federal Reserve's Economic Policy Uncertainty Index for trade jumping to a near record high (Display).

Uncertainty over Economic and Trade Policy Has Spiked

Economic Policy Uncertainty Index: Categorical Index - Trade Policy

When dramatic news upends markets, investors may lose confidence in strategic investments. Yet, moments like these also create fertile ground for active investors to stay focused on the businesses best positioned to benefit from durable trends.

AI and Energy Security: Myths and Facts

The energy transition is a good example. DeepSeek's breakthrough has raised questions about the future trajectory of investment in the transition to a more robust domestic power system and US energy security. What's the connection? Perhaps, some argue, lower development costs of energy-intensive AI technology might reduce the investment needed to improve and upgrade US energy infrastructure below previous forecasts.

We think that conclusion misses the mark because most of the projected rise in energy and power infrastructure investment has nothing to do with AI. In fact, energy investment is primarily being driven by the need to modernize an aging US power grid and to ensure that generation capacity can support more US manufacturing and consumer demand. And rising demand for US power requires secure energy sources.

US power demand is projected to grow by about 2.5% a year through 2030, according to Goldman Sachs's estimates (Display), with AI accounting for about a fifth of total growth. As we see it, this implies that even if AI growth falls short of expectations, power demand will still probably grow by at least 2% a year. So continued investment in upgrading US energy infrastructure and securing supply is still vital. If AI spending exceeds expectations, it will make a strong case for energy security even more compelling, in our view.

US Power Demand Is Poised for Growth Regardless of AI Trajectory

What types of businesses could benefit? We believe companies that manufacture equipment or infrastructure for electrification should continue to enjoy solid growth as the quest for energy security plays out. Similarly, equipment makers for industries like natural gas and nuclear energy, which are essential for meeting higher demand, should also see steady gains.

Tariffs and Trade Wars Buoy Supply Chain Investment

President Trump has been pressing ahead with plans to impose tariffs on Canada, Mexico and China, as well as a wider set of trading partners. The situation is fluid, but the brewing trade war has compounded concerns about the broader industrial economy and levels of capital investment needed to restore US infrastructure.

While the anxiety is understandable, we think the trade war will actually add another catalyst for US companies to secure supply chains, which in turn, will likely bolster infrastructure investment. For many companies, this is already happening. Since the COVID-19 pandemic disrupted global supply chains, companies have been reconfiguring supply chains to reduce the risk of painful production bottlenecks. Concerns about US-China trade tensions have already led many US companies that depended on Chinese suppliers to find alternate sources of supply elsewhere.

Massive spending to secure supply chains is taking place in areas tied to US national security and economic priorities, such as in the semiconductor industry. If anything, the latest round of tariffs has incentivized companies to invest even more to reduce supply chain risk.

Decaying US infrastructure is an obstacle to this process. For years, chronic underinvestment in US ports, airports, roads and power has been a festering problem, which must be addressed in any strategic policy agenda aimed at revitalizing American manufacturing and securing supply chains. Yet in January, Trump sought to eliminate or cut certain components of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act of 2021. In our view, infrastructure investment remains an area of rare bipartisan agreement. So after the dust settles, we expect spending on key initiatives to resume, given the urgency.

Despite near-term policy concerns, recent earning trends indicate that some industries are poised to benefit from the push to secure supply chains. Semiconductor equipment is showing signs of recovery even as sales to China slow, based on revenue from Lam Research, one of the industry's leaders (Display). Logistics in general - and trucking in particular - are stabilizing following a steep decline from pandemic-driven peaks. Spending on cybersecurity continues to power ahead through the turbulence we have seen in the other parts of the economy.

Fortifying Supply Chains: Which Industries May Benefit?

For equity investors, security-oriented themes offer a path to capturing attractive sources of long-term returns in an uncertain environment.

Start by identifying industries that are well positioned to capitalize on US efforts to achieve energy independence and supply chain security. Then, use fundamental research to find companies that are exposed to the themes with shares that trade at a discount because of the uncertainty.

To be sure, investors must be attuned to the rapidly changing policy risks. But with a thematic approach to energy and supply chain security, and an emphasis on quality businesses with competitive advantages, we think investors can create an equity portfolio that is positioned to surmount market volatility and to capture resilient sources of long-term return potential.

References to specific securities discussed are for illustrative purposes only and are not to be considered recommendations by AllianceBernstein L.P.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

data.title: Agnico Eagle Mines Limited: A Long-Term Hold For A Class Act
data.description:
Introduction

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (NYSE:AEM) is the mining operation I would own if I could only own one mine.

Agnico-Eagle is large successful gold mining company and it has been analyzed so often as to now have a high profile in the world of precious metals producers. I have traded this stock since the 1980s and it has been good to me. However on some occasions I have looked to make a purchase but low-balled the bid to hopefully get a bargain and have failed to acquire any shares. The reason that I own shares in Agnico-Eagle is that they came into my hands by the merger and acquisitions route. On the 12th of February, 2020 I was a shareholder in Detour Gold Corp. (DRGDF) when they were acquired by Kirkland Lake Gold Limited (KL) (TSX:KL) and as I liked both companies I held onto the stock. On the 8th February 2022, along came Agnico-Eagle and merged with Kirkland Lake Gold Ltd. Although I thought Agnico’s shares were expensive at the time at around $52.00 I decided to stick with them given the quality and the size of the company who were well positioned to do well in the upcoming Bull Market in the precious metals space. Agnico-Eagle has since flirted with the $100.00 price tag and so I need to decide if it is a Buy, a Sell or a Hold and just how would I implement such a decision.

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited: Brief Description

Agnico Eagle Mines is a $49.25B gold mining company founded in 1957 and has since grown into one of three largest gold producers in the world alongside Newmont Corporation (NEM) and Barrick Gold Corp (GOLD) Traditionally gold producers have rarely paid out a dividend whereas Agnico has consistently paid out a cash dividend since 1983 which is no mean feat especially when times where very hard for this industry. The following picture is an ariel view of one of Agnico’s mines looking a tad cold and covered in snow.

They have their headquarters in Toronto, Canada from where they manage operations in Canada, Australia, Mexico, and Finland, from which their income is derived.

Financials

We will commence with a quick look at the highlights of its fourth quarter and full year 2024 results. I tend to look at those metrics that are important to me and are of value to my decision-making process but I suggest that you read them thoroughly and satisfy yourself that you have a good understanding of Agnico’s ability to perform before you hit the acquisition trail.

One would expect that the spokesperson for the company would have something positive to say about the company and Ammar Al-Joundi, the President and Chief Executive Officer, stated the following:

"I'm pleased to report on another year of record operational and financial performance, achieving our production and cost guidance. This success, along with capital discipline, has enabled us to reduce net debt by $1.3 billion since the beginning of the year and return close to $1.0 billion dollars to our shareholders,"

This is a positive introduction and it suggests that investors have placed their funds with Agnico are safe hands.

One of the metrics that I check is the all-in sustaining costs ("AISC") achieved which is $1,230/Oz on an annual basis, compared with $1,179/Oz for the year ending 2023. But it should be noted that for this quarter they were $1,316/Oz so this is significant increase and something we need to be cognizant of.

This increase is due to higher cash costs per ounce along with sustaining capital expenditures mainly due to their operations at Canadian Malartic, Goldex and Kittila mine sites.

Other standouts that interest me include:

The Payable gold production was 847,401ounces for the quarter and 3,485,336 ounces for 2024 which is as per the company’s previous guidance. This is important to investors that production comes in close to their guidance figures as failure to do so can lead to a loss in investor confidence, a withdrawal of investor funds and a drop in the stock price. For the year of 2024 operating activities generated cash of $3,961 million along with a free cash flow of $2,143 million, which I think is a good result. There is also an increase in gold reserves of 0.9% to 54.3 million ounces which doesn’t sound like a lot but for a large operator it is important to keep the pipeline full thus enabling mining for years to come.

Now we come to debt reduction and maybe it’s because I’m ‘old school’ that this is something that I like to see as during the fourth quarter a sum of $325 million was repaid.

More good news is that for investors looking for an income from their holdings is that Agnico announced a quarterly dividend of $0.40 per share. Another plus is that they bought back 248,700 common shares at an average share price of $80.39, which not only reduces the stock available for purchasers but also looks to be a good move given that the current share price is flirting with the $100.00 share price.

Stable and continuous production is a vital element when viewing any company’s future prospects and this update includes a new three-year estimate which is a tad lower previously stated. This is due largely to the deferral of processing low margin ore. This is not something that I will lose sleep over as the expectation is to produce 3.3 to 3.5 million ounces annually from 2025 to 2027.

I do urge you to read these reports thoroughly and gain a good appreciation of Agnico’s value as I tend to look at those metrics that have an impact on my investment criteria, which are different from yours as we all have differing financial goals, aversion to risk etc.

A quirk of mine is to observe the after-hour trading after the issuance of reports such as this one and to my surprise the stock price fell from $100.81 to touch $97.00. A knee jerk reaction may be due to the revenue being reported a tad lower than expectations. Anyway by 8pm the price had recovered to trade at $100.35. The next week or so will give us an indication of just how the investment community feels about these figures. And of course the movement in the price of gold will feed into all the producers involved in the precious metals space.

For a forward projection, a quick look at Seeking Alpha's Quant section has a PE ('FWD') of 24.06 and an EPS ('FWD') of 4.14, with a rating of “Hold”

Agnico is ranked in its Industry at 16 out of 41 with a score of 3.30 which places Agnico below a number of its peers including Newmont Corporation (NEM) and Barrick Gold Corporation (GOLD)

The liquidity is good with an average volume of 2,682,708 shares traded per day enabling short-term traders to enter and exit this stock with relative ease.

Agnico Eagle is traded on both the NYSE under the ticker symbol of AEM and in Canada under the symbol of AEM.TO.

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited: One Year Chart

Agnico has more than doubled from a low of $44.00 in February 2024 to $100.00 of late.

The RSI and the MACD have peaked in the overbought zone suggesting that breather is due.

A number of investors are now sitting on handsome profits and given that this stock is technically in the overbought zone the temptation to bank those profits will be difficult to resist and so some selling may ensue over the next week or so.

Stock Comparison Chart

Agnico is one the biggest gold miners in the world alongside Newmont Corporation (NEM) and Barrick Gold Corp (GOLD) and the following chart depicts how all three faired over a one-year period.

As we can see Agnico more than doubled over that time frame, which is a considerable achievement for a large market cap company. There are of course gold mining enterprises with a smaller market capitalization that have performed even better than Agnico such as Kinross Gold Corp (KGC), which has a market capitalization of around $14.8B and it gained around 131% over the same period.

A Quick Look At The Chart Of Gold Prices

Note the formation of a Triple Top of $2800/Oz, and that gold has rallied above it to a new all-time high

The RSI is high suggesting that gold is now overbought and may correct in the near-term. Gold’s rally started in March 2024 and peaked in November 2024. Having taken a short breather gold has once again rallied to higher ground. The MACD has formed a positive crossover but it is close to the top of its range suggesting a pullback may be imminent

The Risks that are worthy of some consideration

Agnico's fortunes are largely dependent upon gold's ability to continue to rally to higher ground. Gold has a number of competitors for investors funds including the stock market in general, the US dollar, the crypto market, real estate, etc. which all have various merits and attract large amounts of capital.

Other risks would include but are not limited to changes brought about by the governing bodies in each of their mining jurisdictions. A new government can impose new restrictions which could result in a larger share of their profits being retained by the host nation.

Mining is also fraught with various difficulties and I would like to use the following example to demonstrate an event that can occur at any time. On 11th October 2011 Agnico announced that they had to write off a project known as the Goldex Mine. Below is the chart we used in this article accompanied with the following narrative:

“News that the Goldex Mine is to be written off as hammered Agnico-Eagle Mines Limited (NYSE:AEM) today. The loss is estimated to be around $1.00 per share, however, investors moved fast to dump the stock, taking it down $10.58 or 18.55% on volume of 13 million shares traded in today's session on the NYSE. If ever we needed a reminder of just how difficult a mining operation can be, this is it.”

This sort of disaster doesn’t happen very often but when it does it can severely dent your portfolio as these events do take some time from which to recover.

So as always go gently with your investments and ensure that you can sleep at night even when some of your stocks head south.

Conclusion

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited has more than doubled over the last twelve months proving that it is a class act in the precious metals space.

The technical analysis suggests that Agnico is currently overbought and a near term correction could be imminent. You could protect your gains by selling some of your stock and banking the profits or you could buy a few Put Options which would be the beneficiary of a sudden pullback in Agnico’s share price.

At the moment I am content to hold this position as I am of the opinion that the Bull Market in precious metals has a long way to go and this stock will be one the market leaders.

On two occasions have given this stock a ‘Strong Buy’ rating and I have no reason to down grade it so it remains a ‘Strong Buy’ for me.

Your comments are very much appreciated whether you agree with me or not, so please fire them in, and I will do my best to address each and every one of them.

Go gently.
